title: Whole-Home Repairs Act of 2025
stage_label: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.
plain_summary: The Whole-Home Repairs Act of 2025 creates a new test program to help people fix up their homes. The program gives free money called grants to homeowners who qualify and loans to small landlords who rent out properties. People can use this money to make their homes safer, easier to use for people with disabilities, and better at saving energy. This program is designed to help both homeowners and small property owners make important repairs they might not be able to afford otherwise.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Whole-Home Repairs Act of two thousand twenty-five.

sections.title: Section 2 Definitions
sections.text: In this law, these words mean the following things. An affordable unit is a rental home where the rent is low enough for someone who makes eighty percent or less of the typical income in their area. The Secretary will decide exactly what this means. An assisted unit is a rental home that gets fixed up through this whole-home repairs program run by an organization. An eligible homeowner is someone who owns their home and meets certain income rules. Their household income must be no more than eighty percent of the typical income in their area, or no more than two hundred percent of the federal poverty level, or meet the income rules of another federal program that helps families with limited money. They must also be the official owner according to public records and live in the home as their main residence, or own a manufactured home they live in, or be able to prove they own the property even if they inherited it. An eligible landlord is someone who owns fewer than ten rental properties with mostly affordable units and no more than fifty total units that they or their family controls. They must also agree to follow certain rules described later in this law. An eligible rental property is a home that is rented out or only offered for rent as someone's main place to live. A forgivable loan is money lent to an eligible landlord that is backed by a legal claim on the property. The organization can forgive this loan within three years after the repairs are done if the landlord follows all the loan rules. An implementing organization is a local government or state that will run this whole-home repairs program through one of their agencies or departments. They can also work with local governments or qualified nonprofit groups to run the program. This does not include duplicate organizations in areas already served by someone else getting money under this program. An Indian Tribe has the same meaning as defined in the Native American Housing Assistance and Self-Determination Act of nineteen ninety-six. A qualified nonprofit is a nonprofit organization that has gotten funding from certain government programs like Community Development Block Grants or HOME Investment Partnerships, or has done weatherization or home repair work for at least two years, or has been certified by the Environmental Protection Agency to do lead or energy efficiency work. The Secretary means the Secretary of Housing and Urban Development. A State includes all fifty states, Washington DC, Puerto Rico, any US territory, and Indian Tribes. Whole-home repairs means changes, fixes, or updates to homes where people live to help with accessibility for people with disabilities and older adults like bathroom changes or ramps, safety problems like making sure homes are safe to live in, energy and water efficiency, and other conditions the Secretary decides on.

sections.title: Section 3 Pilot Program
sections.text: The Secretary must start a test program within one year after this law is passed. This program will give money to organizations so they can run whole-home repair programs for eligible homeowners and landlords. Organizations that get money from this program must give grants to eligible homeowners to fix their homes up to a maximum amount per home. This maximum should reflect how much construction costs in that area and must be calculated by the organization and approved by the Secretary. They must also give loans that might be forgiven to eligible landlords to fix affordable rental units and common areas up to a maximum amount that reflects local costs and is approved by the Secretary. Organizations must work with other federal, state, and local home repair programs to help the most people and avoid doing the same work twice. They must make sure all repairs are finished and if not, the loan or grant must be paid back based on how much work was actually done. Any unused money must be returned. Organizations can use up to ten percent of their money for related work like training workers and up to ten percent for administrative costs. They must follow federal accessibility rules and civil rights laws. When making loan agreements with landlords, organizations must include rules that the landlord will follow accessibility and civil rights laws. If the landlord rents to tenants getting rental assistance, they must follow those program rules. If not, they must offer to extend current tenant leases for at least three years on current terms except for rent increases, keep units affordable if tenants move out, prove the property meets building codes, state they have no serious violations of renter protections in the past ten years, and limit rent increases to five percent or inflation whichever is lower for at least three years. Organizations wanting money must apply with information about where they will work, how they will pick suborganizations if needed, how they will coordinate with other programs, data showing housing need, how they will process applications, and other information the Secretary needs. When choosing who gets money, the Secretary will prioritize state applications that plan to work in every area and give rural areas their fair share of money. The Secretary wants to pick organizations from different types of places like cities, suburbs, rural areas, and tribal lands. Organizations that got money before can still apply again. The Secretary will provide information about other federal programs that recipients can coordinate with. Each year, the Secretary will give money to at least two but no more than ten organizations and no more than one per state. If a loan is not forgiven, that money can be used again for new grants or loans. This money must add to, not replace, other federal, state, and local money. The Secretary should try to make programs work better together and reduce the burden on applicants by recognizing income eligibility decisions made in the past year for similar programs. Organizations getting money must submit yearly reports with information about how many homes they served, average costs and types of repairs, number of applications received and processed, demographic information about who got help, and a plan to prevent waste and fraud. The Secretary should try to make reporting requirements similar to other programs organizations might also use. The Inspector General must assess the program at least twice during the pilot period. The Secretary must send yearly summary reports to Congress. The Secretary can use up to twenty-five million dollars from existing funds for programs run by the Office of Lead Hazard Control and Healthy Homes to pay for this pilot program. This pilot program will end on October first, two thousand thirty.
